issue_title: API: get_params()
issue_body:
As observed in #141 (but also in #110 and https://github.com/benanne/Lasagne/issues/136#issuecomment-75169847), it's not entirely clear what `Layer.get_params()` means and what it should mean.

The documentation of `get_params()` says: "Returns a list of all the Theano variables that parameterize the layer.", and further below it says "it should be overridden in a subclass that has trainable parameters".
In addition, we have `get_bias_params()`, which is documented as: "Returns a list of all the Theano variables that are bias parameters for the layer.", and a note further below says "This is useful when specifying regularization (it is often undesirable to regularize bias parameters)."

Now when we include the use case of Batch Normalization, we actually have three overlapping classes of variables:

1. variables involved in the forward pass (currently `get_params()` according to its docstring)
2. variables to be updated wrt. the loss function (currently `get_params()` according to its usage in Lasagne)
3. variables to be updated wrt. the loss function that are not to be included in weight regularization (currently `get_bias_params()`)

Before the first release of Lasagne we should re-think the `get_params` and `get_bias_params` API. It obviously does not exactly match what comes up in the use cases. Should we possibly replace this with a single `get_params()` method with optional keyword arguments to filter out certain things? Most Layer classes would not need to bother with the keywords or just react on the bias-related keyword, so it wouldn't increase the complexity of adding a new Layer class. Shall we think that through?

issue_title: Unable to implement LSTM weight cloning/sharing
issue_body:
Hey, 

For training sequence to sequence RNNs it is very good to have batches as random as possible. So for a event based (only look at the output at a fixed time point) training I want to have single output for training and a continuous (sequence to sequence) output for testing my LSTM layer. It would therefore be necessary to share the weigths of two LSTM layers. I tried to implement this as similar as possible to  to the lasagne example and according to the concept of theano shared variables for the DenseLayer but it did not work out... Attached a plug and play code of what I'm trying to do. Thanks for any help and thanks for Lasagne! 

```
import lasagne
import numpy as np

def lstm_weight_sharing():

    grad_clipping = 0
    n_hidden = 8    # number of hidden units
    batch_size = 9
    sequence_len = 10
    n_feat = 11     # number of features

    # this is how I intuitively think it should be done stolen from the example code in lasagne
    l_in_dense = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((batch_size, n_feat))
    l_train = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(l_in_dense, n_hidden)
    l_test = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(l_in_dense, n_hidden, W=l_train.W, b=l_train.b)

    print [[param_train.get_value() == param_test.get_value()] for param_train, param_test in
           zip(lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_train), lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_test))]  #all True

    forget_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()     # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    input_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()      # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    output_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()     # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    cell = lasagne.layers.Gate(W_cell=None, nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.tanh)

    # sequence input layer
    l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((batch_size, sequence_len, n_feat))

    # sequ to label layer, only return final. This is for more representative, flexible training
    l_LSTM_train = lasagne.layers.LSTMLayer(l_in, n_hidden,
                                           nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.tanh,
                                           grad_clipping=grad_clipping,
                                           hid_init=lasagne.init.Constant(0.),  # to make sure the behave the same
                                           only_return_final=True,
                                           ingate=input_gate, outgate=output_gate, forgetgate=forget_gate,
                                           cell=cell)

    # copy of the layer above but sequ to sequ for testing
    l_LSTM_test = lasagne.layers.LSTMLayer(l_in, n_hidden,
                                           nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.tanh,
                                           grad_clipping=grad_clipping,
                                           hid_init=lasagne.init.Constant(0.),  # to make sure the behave the same
                                           only_return_final=False,
                                           ingate=input_gate, outgate=output_gate, forgetgate=forget_gate,
                                           cell=cell)

    l_LSTM_test.W_in_to_cell = l_LSTM_train.W_in_to_cell  # treat them like theano shared variables
    l_LSTM_test.b_cell = l_LSTM_train.b_cell  # treat them like theano shared variables
    l_LSTM_test.W_hid_to_cell = l_LSTM_train.W_hid_to_cell  # treat them like theano shared variables

    print 'some params of LSTM layer train'
    print [[param, param.get_value()[0]] for param in lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_LSTM_train)]

    print 'some params of LSTM layer test'  # they should look like the ones of LSTM_train but don't...
    print [[param, param.get_value()[0]] for param in lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_LSTM_test)]
```

issue_title: Better Money support
issue_body:
Now we're have some problems, which are related to using of `moneyed.Money`.

### F expressions support is not complete

This is possible:

```python
F('field') + Money(1, 'EUR')
```

But this one will rise ``TypeError``:

```python
Money(1, 'EUR') + F('field')
```

```TypeError: Cannot add or subtract a Money and non-Money instance.```

The ``moneyed`` package should not know about Django's ``F`` expressions, and we need to implement it on the `django-money` side to fix the issue.

### Lacking of clarity about MoneyPatched

IMO the name doesn't clarify the purpose of the class. Sometimes we're using it, sometimes not, but it is not clear why.

### What to do?

Use only `Money` from `djmoney`, not from `moneyed`, which will be a subclass of ``moneyed.Money`` and will contain all additional logic to support Django. 
If we will use this class internally and encourage users to use it, than we will need only one entity instead of two. In this case we can guarantee, that all Django features will be supported by this class, we can't do it for ``moneyed.Money``.
Probably, we should support ``moneyed.Money`` as we do now, but add a note, that in this case some features are not supported.

So, my proposal is:

- Rename ``MoneyPatched`` -> ``Money``
- Add complete ``F`` expressions support
- Replace all usages of ``moneyed.Money`` to ``djmoney.Money``
- Update documentation accordingly

@benjaoming , what do you think?

issue_title: Symbolic Expression of register states
Potential bug with step when dealing with a self modifying binary
issue_body:
The [Zwiebel](https://github.com/angr/angr-doc/tree/master/examples/tumctf2016_zwiebel) example gives some odd behavior in angr. 

```
0x601310:	lea	esi, dword ptr [rsi]
0x601316:	lea	esi, dword ptr [rsi]
0x60131a:	lea	esi, dword ptr [rsi]
0x601321:	lea	edi, dword ptr [rdi]
0x601328:	mov	rax, rbx
0x60132b:	mov	al, byte ptr [rax]
0x60132e:	and	al, 0x40
0x601330:	je	0x601348
```

starting off at 0x601310 and running `simgr.step()` results in a state whose basic block is this:
```
0x2000001:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
0x2000003:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
0x2000005:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
0x2000007:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
0x2000009:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
0x200000b:    add    byte ptr [rax], al
```

if you instead run `simgr.step(num_inst=8)` or `simgr.step(num_inst=7); simgr.step()` you get the expected result with 2 active states.

Here is the script I am using:
```
import angr
import claripy

proj = angr.Project("zwiebel", support_selfmodifying_code=True, load_options={'auto_load_libs': False})

obj_shc = 0x601310

state = proj.factory.blank_state(addr=obj_shc, add_options = angr.options.unicorn)

#rbx holds the input flag
flag = claripy.BVS('flag', 64*8)
flag_location = 0x1000
state.memory.store(flag_location, flag)
state.regs.rbx = claripy.BVV(flag_location, 64)

simgr = proj.factory.simulation_manager(state)

#simgr.step() #this fails
#simgr.step(num_inst=8) #this works
#simgr.step(num_inst=7); simgr.step() #this works
```

issue_title: Proposal: New area file format
issue_body:
This is a proposal for pyresample to support a new file format to get area definitions from. There are a lot of decisions that could go in to something like this so I wanted to make an issue before I threw something together and have everyone hate it. Depending on what gets decided the current format may stay around, but my hope is that the new format is so good no one will want to use the old format anymore. Also this new format may be made most flexible if there is a method or function or class that provides most of the convenience that the configuration provides (see below). What this would mean for the current classes and methods, I'm not sure.

I've been thinking about this for a while now during satpy development because satpy will be heavily used by Polar2Grid and therefore Polar2Grid's users. Polar2Grid has its own comma-separated way of specifying "grids" and I'd like to use whatever format satpy uses, but I'm not really a fan of the current format nor does the current format provide all of the features that P2G's provides. These features revolve around the idea of convenience for the user and allowing them to enter what they want or what they know and having logical defaults fill in the gaps. In P2G's case, some users don't know what projections are or don't know that meters in LCC are different than meters in Mercator.

NOTE: All areas have a `name` and an optional `description`. The `name` must be unique among all other areas in the file.

Ways Areas could be specified but not limited just to these:

1. Extents - Outer pixel edge coordinates in meters (legacy spec)
 a. PROJ.4 String (or dictionary, debatable)
 b. 4-element list of extents in projection units (same as current format)
 c. number of columns and rows in pixels

2. Geotiff specification
 a. PROJ.4 string or dict
 b. upper-left origin (X, Y) in projection units
 c. Cell size (X, Y) in projection units (a.k.a. pixel resolution)
 d. number of columns and rows in pixels

3. Circle - resulting area is still rectangular
 a. PROJ.4 string or dict
 b. center coordinate (X, Y) in projection units
 c. radius in projection units

4. Area of interest - Geotiff based on center point
 a. Center coordinate (X, Y) in projection units
 b. Cell size (X, Y) in projection units
 c. number of columns and rows in pixels
 d. PROJ.4 string or dict

5. Swath Definition - Maybe?
 a. binary longitude file (.npz is numpy array, .dat is float32 binary, etc?)
 b. binary latitude file

In addition to this I think it should be possible for a user to specify anything that is in "projection units" in degrees except for maybe cell size and radius. I've often helped people who started using pyresample and one of their first questions is "What are these 4 numbers for extents? How do I calculate them?". Allowing for someone to enter degrees would help with that.

Another feature would be logical defaults. For example, in number 3 or 4 the PROJ.4 could be optional and a "best projection" could be chosen based on the absolute value of the latitude of the center point (ex. mercator for < 15, lcc for <60, polar stereographic otherwise).

Another feature that would require support inside of pyresample would be "dynamic grids" as they are called in Polar2Grid. These are grids where things like origin or number of cols/rows or even cell size is determined by the data being resampled. Based on my experience in Polar2Grid with this an important thing that can be overlooked is that datasets that are being resampled for the same geographic scene (time and location) should usually share the same resolved dynamic Area. Most likely this resolved Area used the highest resolution input swath definition to determine things. This is very important if the resampled results are going to be compared or merged in to a composite. In Polar2Grid this resolution step of dynamic areas is hidden inside the resampling code. This probably wouldn't work best for pyresample since it limits what the user can do with the resulting area.

Lastly, what format would best serve something like this:
1. YAML (my vote)
2. INI
3. Other?

issue_title: hostname resolution broken
issue_body:
nearly all LHCb users  (and probably many more not LHCb) (regardless of site etc) see their hostname stored as `localhost`.

This is due to the following exception being caught in a catch all exception handler:
``` WARNING  NameError: global name 'hostname_tmp' is not defined ```

This is thrown from:
https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/develop/python/Ganga/Utility/util.py#L160

I suspect this is because the object simply isn't defined.

Why we're simply not using `socket.getfqdn` I don't know but I'll prepare a PR for this.

This has ruined the monitoring data and messes up the jobs summary such that #383 is an issue.

This should probably have been caught a _long_ time ago.

This isn't serious but I will get this into 6.2.1 as this is slightly bad. If there are any more PR before 6.2.0 I'd like to bump this for a few reasons.

issue_title: Not compatible with Python 3
Support for Python 3.6
issue_body:
### Version of the SDK being used
```bash
$ pip freeze | grep face
facebook-sdk==2.0.0
```

### Expected Behavior
```python
>>> import facebook
>>> # the next line should not raise an error
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
>>> # yay!
```

### Actual behavior
```python
>>> import facebook
>>> # the next line should not raise an error
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'facebook' has no attribute 'GraphAPI'
```

### Steps to Reproduce
This *only* happens in Python 3.6. Watch this:

```bash
$ workon my-35-virtualenv
(my-35-virtualenv) $ python --version
3.5.2
(my-35-virtualenv) $ python
>>> import facebook
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
>>> # yay!
>>> exit(0)
(my-35-virtualenv) $ workon my-36-virtualenv
(my-36-virtualenv) $ python --version
3.6.1
(my-36-virtualenv) $ python
>>> import facebook
>>> facebook.GraphAPI
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'facebook' has no attribute 'GraphAPI'
```

This leads me to believe that python 3.6 changed how imports work. I'll do some research on 3.6 imports, but I wanted this issue to be known. From what I saw there aren't any issues here nor on the facebook developer forum that address this.

issue_title: "Security Considerations" section in the docs.
issue_body:
We identified several issues which would be best practices when deploying a Kinto server:

* Use bucket id's that are unique to the group that has access to the collections in that bucket (i.e. don't use a "default" bucket that would make data from different users use identical URLs).
* Restrict the `"access-control-allow-origin"` header if the allowed origin(s) is/are known.
* Send a `"Cache-Control": "no-cache"` header on each response (this does not prevent caching, but it makes sure the browser cache does not leak info without triggering a revalidate and checking authorization with the server first.

There might be more things worth adding to this section.

issue_title: Cannot parse HTTP headers without white space after colon in SSDP response
issue_body:
ssdp.py fails to parse HTTP headers like;
`Location:http://192.168.1.1:1900/`
when there is no white space after the first colon(:).
HTTP 1.1 allows 0 or more white spaces after the first colon.

This is caused by regular expression in line 19 of ssdp.py
`RESPONSE_REGEX` = re.compile(r'\n(.*)\: (.*)\r')
which requires 1 (and only 1) white space to be present.

This regular expression should be changed to
`RESPONSE_REGEX` = re.compile(r'\n(.*?)\:\s*(.*)\r')
to allow arbitrary number of white spaces.

issue_title: Reflection bug on SQLAlchemy 1.2
issue_body:
Hi there,

It looks like SQLAlchemy has changed a few method signatures in version >=1.2.  The following statement works on SQLAlchemy <1.2 but not on SQLAlchemy >= 1.2:

I get the following traceback with SQLAlchemy version 1.2.5:

    table = sa.Table(table_name, meta, **table_kwargs)

    metadata._remove_table(name, schema)

  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/sql/schema.py", line 533, in _init
    include_columns, _extend_on=_extend_on)
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/sql/schema.py", line 559, in _autoload
    _extend_on=_extend_on
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/engine/base.py", line 2056, in run_callable
    return conn.run_callable(callable_, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/engine/base.py", line 1545, in run_callable
    return callable_(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/engine/default.py", line 389, in reflecttable
    table, include_columns, exclude_columns, **opts)
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/engine/reflection.py", line 618, in reflecttable
    table_name, schema, **table.dialect_kwargs):
  File "/home/colin/.virtualenvs/spectrify/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sqlalchemy/engine/reflection.py", line 369, in get_columns
    **kw)

TypeError: _get_column_info() takes exactly 9 arguments (8 given)

issue_title: Async/Await keywords with AyncioExecutor
issue_body:
I'm running into a bug using the `async`/`await` keywords and the AsyncioExecutor. Here is my test:

```python

from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
import graphene

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):

    recipes = graphene.String()

    async def resolve_recipes(self, *_):
        return 'hello'


schema = graphene.Schema(

    executor=AsyncioExecutor()
)

executed = schema.execute("""

issue_title: Graphene-SqlAlchemy Inheritance Support
issue_body:
Hi, this library has been very useful in creating API's. 

Does it support single table inheritance? I have a simple example based on [this](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/inheritance.html). But when I try to query this schema, I am only able to see the base classes fields in the graphiql console. It seems that graphene-sqlalchemy did not pick up the existence of the fields at all.

```
class GroupedObject(db.Model):
    __tablename__ = "grouped_object"
    id = db.Column(db.String(64), primary_key=True)
    name = db.Column(db.String(64), nullable=False)
    children = db.relationship('GroupedObject', secondary="groups", lazy="joined",
                               primaryjoin=id == Group.parent_id,
                               secondaryjoin=id == Group.child_id,
                               backref="parents")

    def add_child(self, child, activation_time=None):
        db.session.merge(Group(self.id, child.id, activation_time))
        db.session.commit()

    def __repr__(self):
        return json.dumps({"id": self.id, "name": self.name, "children": self.children})

class TestModel(GroupedObject):
    """
    Represents operator table
    """

    __tablename__ = "operator"
    id = db.Column(db.String(64), db.ForeignKey('grouped_object.id'), primary_key=True)

    def __init__(self, model_id, name):
        self.id = model_id
        self.name = name

class TestSchema(SQLAlchemyObjectType):                                                                                                                                            
    class Meta:                                                                                                                                                                  
        model = TestModel
```

Is there any method of establishing this structure?

issue_title: MTD vrs FTD builds, and Makefile.am If/Else/Endif issues
issue_body:
As discussed in #1467 (Changes to Support IDEs)

These problems are intertwined and share a number of common traits and are being addressed together in a single commit, sadly I don't see a way to break this up.

**PROBLEM 1**   
In src/core/Makefile.am - lists numerous IF/ELSE/ENDIF which choose which files are added or not added to a specific library. While IDEs can provide some level of exclusion, they are not that flexible.

**PROBLEM 2**  
Need both --enable-cli-ftd --enable-cli-mtd, and likewise --enable-mtd

Need granularity such that CLI can be built with MTD only, And NCP can be built using MTD or FTD
Current ./configure solution is: FTD for both, or FTD for Neither

(there are no details for this item below)

**PROBLEM 3**
Linking the CLI application with the CLI library, and NCP with the NCP library

Currently when the CLI (or NCP) library is built - it is built using only *one* of the two internal header configurations, ie: FTD - with all of the structure/class elements, or  MTD with various structure/class elements removed.

This causes problems with link time optimization and static code analysis.

**PROBLEM 4**
Appvayor.

I do not have access to the version of Visual Studio used here, I have Visual Studio 2015, but the project appears to use an older version of Visual Studio, my experience has been:  VS2015 will upgrade the projects... this is a one-way process  

if someone can advise, it would be helpful.

DETAILS FOLLOW

================================================

**PROBLEM 1 - details**

a)  Makefile.am files should not longer use If/Else/Endif type constructs to add/remove files from a build.
Instead, the makefile should generally compile everything.

For example some IDEs - such as Eclipse CDT based IDEs - compile all files "*.c" and "*.cpp" and are of the view - if the file does not belong, or should not be compiled then it should not be present in the directory.  While other IDEs have other schemes, in IDE land - there does not exist a single common way to exclude/include files based upon the build configuration.

**Solution:** Where required, appropriate #if/#else/#endif type statements need to be added to various source files so that the entire quote "translation unit" - effectively becomes a pseudo-empty translation unit.

A number of files did not have, or do not #include "openthread-config.h" - which is required to support the #if/#else/#endif described above, this was added to a number of files.

Result: A number of places additional tests must be applied to the conditional.

For example in several places, the #if SOMETHING becomes: #if SOMETHING && OPENTHREAD_FTD

**PROBLEM 2** Details

This is described above, not expanded here.

**PROBLEM 3** Details

When the NCP or CLI library is built, it uses the internal thread headers, and thus structures and class members.

When the NCP or CLI application is linked, the openthread library effectively uses a different set of header files (either MTD or FTD configuration) - the net result is these internal structures are different.

What is needed is the CLI library must be built in FTD mode, and MTD mode so that it matches the openthread library, these differences are flagged as errors by compilers and static code analysis tools that do "link-time-optimization"

Thus, in all cases we have added two command line defines:

 When building anything-FTD,  "-DOPENTHREAD_FTD=1"  must be defined
When building anything-MTD, "-DOPENTHREAD_MTD=1" must be defined

**PROBLEM 4** - No access to Visual Studio OLD?

I have Visual Studio 2015 - I do not have older versions, I cannot make any of the required changes to support the above for appvayor.

I believe the change is simple, because I believe the Windows solution only builds the FTD configuration.

In the library build projects add:  -DOPENTHREAD_FTD=1

issue_title: Process hangs when pyfftw used within a multiprocessing process
issue_body:
In certain circumstances, pyfftw causes multiprocessing processes to hang, as in the following minimal example:

```
from __future__ import print_function
from builtins import input
from builtins import range

import numpy as np
import pyfftw
import multiprocessing as mp
import logging

mpl = mp.log_to_stderr()
mpl.setLevel(logging.INFO)

nthreads=2

X = np.random.randn(512, 512).astype(np.float32)
Z = pyfftw.interfaces.numpy_fft.fftn(X.astype(np.float64), threads=nthreads)
aX = mp.Array('f', X.ravel())

def fn(b, ax=aX, sh=X.shape):

    X = np.array(ax).reshape(sh)
    Y = pyfftw.interfaces.numpy_fft.fftn(X, threads=nthreads)
    return np.linalg.norm(b*Y)

p = mp.Pool(processes=4)
R = p.map(fn, [1e-2, 1e-1, 1e0, 1e1])
print(R)
```

The example does not hang if `nthreads=1` or if `X.astype(np.float64)` is replaced with `X`. Setting the number of threads to one is a reasonable workaround, but it would be much better if this were not necessary.

issue_title: csvlook bool inference too aggressive
issue_body:
When dealing with csv files with only one row, csvlook converts to boolean each 0 or 1 it finds.
In my opinion this is too aggressive. See also #506.
Example:
```
$ cat test0.csv
core,us
0,60000011
```
```
$ csvlook test0.csv
|  core |         us |
| ----- | ---------- |
| False | 60.000.011 |
```
The same happens with a 1 instead of a 0.

This is with csvkit 1.0.0, from the git repository.

I think this kind of inference can be very problematic, but if done, I would be better to first wait until there is a significant number of rows with only 1 and 0 values. E.g. only try to infer booleans if the number of rows with only 1 or 0 values is > X, where X >> 1. 

However, I would totally get rid of this inference rule.

issue_title: The way DAV figures out names of collections is wrong
issue_body:
Right now each collection has a "name" and a "displayname". They have distinctive properties:

* The range of allowed characters is restricted for "name". This is not specified anywhere, but at least slashes and nullbytes are not allowed. In practice you might not be able to use anything else than `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]`, depending on the DAV server.
* The "name" must be unique among all collections for the user.
* It should not be possible to change the "name" after creating the collection.

For `filesystem`-storage, the "name" is the directory name, and "displayname" is a file inside the directory.

For `caldav`/`carddav`, the "displayname" is just a value attached to the collection, and isn't really important. As per the RFC, collections are uniquely identified by URLs, but we don't use them for "name".

But since those URLs are too long and contain slashes, we do something completely bizarre: We use the *last path segment* for identifying a collection. For most FOSS servers this provides us with a human-readable unique identifier, so at first sight it appeared perfect for use in "name".

Unfortunately, as per the RFC, the last path segment does neither have to be human-readable nor unique:

* Apple's iCloud uses UUIDs in that segment, which are not human-readable.
* For Google CalDAV, the last path segment is just the string `events`, the real (again a UUID, therefore not human-readable) identifier is in the previous one. So we have this behavior changed accordingly in the `google` storage types.

 In the long run we should find a different way to generate "name" for the DAV-storages. I think, if ever, this should be done pre-1.0 as it will come with massive breakage for the user.

issue_title: standalone should allow for alternate ports to bind to (but not authenticate to)

If the `standalone` plugin were to allow users to specify which port to bind to (such as 8080), then it could be run as-needed for `certonly` behavior behind nginx/apache/ or any other server via a proxypass directive.

all challenges should would still be routed through port 80 (and 443 if needed).  this would just afford the person who owns root privileges some flexibility as to how they route traffic.

issue_title: DNS problem: SERVFAIL looking up A for <domain>.co.uk
issue_body:
Unable to do the email verification (that does the MX record lookup). Or certificate generation (that does the A record lookup).

Both fail.

The server in question is:
* Hosted by Dyn Inc (one of the larges DNS providers).
* Setup and functioning normally, and has been for a long time.
* Using DNSSEC, which is configured and working normally.

The command is:
```
./letsencrypt-auto certonly --standalone --register-unsafely-without-email -d example.co.uk -d www.example.co.uk --agree-tos
```

Doing a `dig a example.co.uk` (using Google Public DNS as the servers resolver), the IP is returned as expected, so the DNS appears to be working correctly. Also, since Google Public DNS supports DNSSEC, the successful resolution indicates that it isn't a DNSSEC issue).

I'm running the command from a Debian 8.3 box, fresh brand new instance on Digital Ocean. The IP for the box is also the domains IP.

Same issue on Fedora 23 when installed by the package manager. On a different server, also on Digital Ocean. Again, a manual `dig` reveals the IP without issue.

issue_title: Check untyped defs in the Apache plugin
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-apache` module.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Organize + document `certbot/tests/testdata` directory
issue_body:
We have a horribly organized `certbot/tests/testdata/` directory, and it's not clear which of our testing certificates go with which keys, and this has caused some confusion. Ideally, this would be documented somewhere around here: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#testing.

Here's a small sample of what's involved in part of this (e.g. checking that a private key goes with a certificate):

```
$ openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in cert.pem | openssl md5
(stdin)= c1cc74880b0f3366db7c9fc48a359dff
$ openssl rsa -noout -modulus -in rsa256_key.pem | openssl md5
(stdin)= f7f1dda5f208c1718c3c18189114ddd6
```

Since the hashes don't match, they don't go together (but those two are used together in a lot of the unit tests).

Relevant preliminary reading on the differences between `fullchain`, `cert`, and `chain` files for whoever takes this on: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#where-are-my-certificates

issue_title: X-Forwarded-For-Proto SSL and redirect behaviour in Nginx
Documentation: Use "certificate" consistently
issue_body: Our documentation uses "certificate" and "cert" interchangeably. For better or worse, many of our flag names do too. We should fix the documentation and the `--help` output to consistently use certificate instead of cert.

issue_title: Mavproxy starts up with only garbage packets - restarts fine
issue_body:
I have a number of vehicles I have built which run Raspberry Pi's hooked up via the serial port to a Pixhawk. Mavproxy is configured to start at boot. Some non-zero percentage of time mavproxy starts up in what I call "garbage mode" and just pumps out noise packets - never connects to autopilot. Restarting mavproxy it comes up properly the second time every time. It seems almost like the baud rate isn't configured properly sometimes. I have dug and dug to see if any other process is opening the serial port and mucking with things, but I haven't found anything. Likewise I have played with the startup timing extensively and it makes no difference.

Has anyone experienced this? Any tips? Considering patching mavproxy to have a "restart on excessive noise" function, but that seems like a bandaid.

issue_title: Optimize rebalancing
issue_body: Right now rebalance process thinks that 1,2,3 and 1,3,2 distribution is different and tries to move it (sometimes to [4,5,6]). Copying data does not makes any sense, so it's better not to make difference between 1,2,3 and 1,3,2 and use (leader_count, overall_partition_count) as optimization strategy

issue_title: Pipeline schedule should support specifying events
issue_body:
The schedule section supports specifying cron expressions. In addition it would be useful to trigger based on events such as data showing up in a specific location. For example, %ml pipeline could begin the preprocessing/training step if a file shows up in GCS. 

Ideally, this is done via PubSub with Composer subscribing to a particular topic, and then firing off the DAG in response to a message (in fact, the whole DAG spec could even be in the payload). 

Optionally, this could be implemented via Airflow's SensorOperator that listens to external events such that:
1. the ML pipeline waits on some external input to start (sensor task)
2. the last task in the ML pipeline is to trigger a new ML pipeline run
3. an ML pipeline is always scheduled and waiting for sensory input.

issue_title: Datalab API libraries should support retries on 5XX errors
issue_body:
Currently, no HTTP requests are ever retried; however, almost all google APIs (including all the ones Datalab talks to) recommend retrying on 5XX errors, though of course each API has slightly different recommendations.

Here's the page with the info for BigQuery retry recommendations:
  https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/troubleshooting-errors

issue_title: [Style proposal] Enforce clearer interface in classes with public, private and protected members
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Right now, Raiden doesn't have a clear, visible, convention-enforced distinction between what methods, attributes and properties can be used to access the main functionality of a class, object or module, and what is/should be used only internally and/or is implementation specific.

This makes first understanding the code pretty hard, as one have to walk through the whole file, plus perform a couple of `grep`s in the codebase in order to understand how, where and in what context any method is used. Onboarding of new developers are also made harder because of that.

Python doesn't have access modifiers for members, but [good conventions](http://radek.io/2011/07/21/private-protected-and-public-in-python/) [are supported](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/classes.html#private-variables) and can bring nice benefits and increase readability greatly.

If this conventions are followed, it should be easier to understand the code, allowing for example easy drawing of the class and hierarchy diagrams for Raiden, which should contain only the public interface of each class. This adds value to the overall code quality, and raise awareness of developers when changing something that can break things currently out of sight.

### Proposal

- All Raiden classes should define and expose the minimum possible public interface (all members, mainly methods, attributes and properties)
- This interface is considered the most important architectural decision and should be changed carefully to avoid breaks
- When possible/needed, shrinking this interface is desired over growing it (by removing itif possible, or making a previously public member protected)
- Other Raiden classes must access instances of this class only through its public interface
- This public interface must be tested, and it may be the only members tested: we may not require non-public interface tests (TBD). Reasoning: private/protected members should be used only from the public interface, so they're tested from this public interface anyway, and are an implementation detail, so they may change without needing to implement/change tests.
- Private/protected members should be the default, and its names start with a `_`.
- Free standing functions and data are considered members of the module: if they're used only inside it, the naming convention above applies. If they're used externally, they may be good candidates of being moved to some common utility module/package (`utils.py`, raiden-libs, etc)
- It may be useful to also enforce a grouping of [non-]public member definitions: e.g. all public methods of a class should be put last, after all private/protected methods, so they can be easily found and walkthrough-ed

### TODO

- [ ] Discuss and decide the convention and update [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md) accordingly
- [ ] Walk through classes, identify its minimum public interface, rename and move members accordingly

    def transform_texts(texts):
        # Load the annotation models
        nlp = English()

        # the GIL around the parser, for efficient multi-threading.
        for doc in nlp.pipe(texts, n_threads=4):
            # Iterate over base NPs, e.g. "all their good ideas"
            for np in doc.noun_chunks:
                # Only keep adjectives and nouns, e.g. "good ideas"
                while len(np) > 1 and np[0].dep_ not in ('amod', 'compound'):

                if len(np) > 1:

                # Iterate over named entities

                        # Merge them into single tokens

            for token in tokens:

issue_title: Improving qcodes.github.io documentation
issue_body:
When introducing new users to QCoDeS, I usually direct them to the [qcodes.github.io](qcodes.github.io\QCoDeS) documentation. However, I have noticed that people often have quite some remaining questions, and what I usually end up doing is performing a few measurements with them, explaining everything along the way.

I browsed through the documentation, and what I feel is missing most is a clear tutorial that explains exactly what a beginning user needs to know to perform his/her first measurement. There is `docs/examples/Tutorial.ipynb`, which does this to some extent, but it's located at the end of the documentation, and I believe it can use some improvements.

I was therefore thinking of rewriting the Tutorial. I think the target audience should be people who have freshly installed QCoDeS, and want to connect their instruments and perform their first measurement.  I've listed my ideas for improving the Tutorial below, please comment if you want things added, or disagree with anything:
- Introduce how the tutorial is structured.
- Reference to other parts of documentation, such as `Parameter`, `Instrument` documentation/definition, other examples, etc.
    This should be possible using nbsphinx. It will make it much easier for users to get more information.
- Shorten code for creating dummy instruments/parameters
    I'm don't think it would be wise to move it entirely to a separate file, as I do see the appeal of introducing instruments and parameters this way. However, it's also a bit distracting, also because we're using mock instruments. I think it can be created easily using just an `Instrument`. A lot of the code can also be removed by using the features of the new `Parameter` class.
- Shorten `Basic instrument interaction`.
    While it's good to mention that parameters have many properties, such as a step, I think actually showing examples of using these properties should be moved elsewhere, or at least be made more succinctly (no 40 print statements).
- Move more text to markdown cells, instead of comments.
    It's more easily readable this way.
- Give short explanation of location provider, and data saving location, move up to after import
- Shorten `dac.snapshot()`.
    While I think it's a good idea to mention that the metadata is stored, having an entire page of metadata is distracting. Not sure how to best improve this. Also it should be moved up to before the first loop.
- Add section for iPython for loop magic, noting that it's for iPython users only.
    Our group now uses this by default, and it definitely makes a measurement more readable. I think it would be a good idea to mention this early on. Thoughts?


Additionally, I think a restructuring of the documentation would help. I propose the following changes:
- Change `Getting started` to `Setting up QCoDeS`
  It doesn't contain information on actually getting started with measurements, which I think is good.
- Adding a tutorial as the next section (see above)
- Move `Get help` to last section, and mention that there is a tutorial + list of examples etc.
- Combine `User guide` and `Community guide`, make Contributing a separate section
- Move `Examples of using QCoDeS` up to below user guide, clean up TOC.
    The TOC should be much more succinct. This will mean either reducing its max-depth, or changing the ipynb files in it. For sure the driver examples should have max-depth=1. Also move some examples to `Advanced examples`

I've also noticed a lot of overlap between the `User guide` (rst) and the examples (ipynb). I'm not sure what the best approach is here, as the user guide can be quite dry, especially without examples. Any thoughts?

@jenshnielsen @WilliamHPNielsen

issue_title: [Discussion] squeezenet model; different num of classes
issue_body:
For training from scratch, the code would work fine. But mostly, people would use the pre-trained weights, so on top of setting the self.num_classes, you also have to change the final_conv layer, after setting pre-trained weights. 

```
        final_conv = nn.Conv2d(512, self.num_classes, kernel_size=1)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Dropout(p=0.5),
            final_conv,
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.AvgPool2d(13))
```

While this may be straightforward in other models, in this the final_conv (local to __init__) is actually inside a sequential module. And when I tried to change only the final_conv layer from self.classifier (by writing classifier[1] = nn.Conv2d etc) I get the error 'Sequential object doesnt support item assignment').

Eventually I just changed the classifier block to a nn.Sequential module with the new conv, reflecting new number of classes.

Few things to ask/discuss:
1. Am I making some mistake in changing only the final_conv layer from inside classifier sequential module.
2. If not, should we change that part so that its easier to just change only the final_conv layer?
3. If the above isn't preferred, should I just add a comment somewhere so that people will know they have to change the classifier itself? 
4. People should be able to figure it out, this is minor :P

3rd option makes sense as they might have to change AvgPool also, but I had removed it and was using the functional API for that, so didnt realize until now.

issue_title: RequestLimitExceeded during cluster autoscaling
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

Sometimes you see RequestLimitExceeded (on DescribeInstances or some other AWS poll) during operation of an autoscaling cluster. This seems to be because we poll very aggressively (every time in the monitor.py loop). We should probably (1) poll AWS less often, and (2) back-off on RequestLimitExceeded exceptions (boto already does some internal backoff, but it doesn't appear to retry for extended periods of time).

issue_title: [RFC] Improve Java API
issue_body:
Hi there. This is Hao Chen from Ant Financial.
 
Recently we had some internal discussions on improving the Java API. Before making any change, we'd like to open a wider discussion here on the possible options. 

If you're interested in using Ray on Java, your feedbacks are very much valuable.  Thanks in advance!

## Option 1: `Ray.call` (Current implementation)
Let's say we have the following method
```java
class Echo {
    public static int echo(int x) { return x; }
}
```
To invoke this method remotely on ray, we can do 
```java
RayObject<Integer> res = Ray.call(Echo::echo, 1);
System.out.println(res.get());
```

### Problems
1. Compared with Python API (`echo.remote(1)`), this way is less natural, and is somewhat against Java convention. 
2. Its internal implementation is a little bit hacky. (The TLDR explanation is that the implementation uses byte-code manipulation to change function's behavior at runtime. I'm not gonna to go too deeply because I'd like to make this discussion more focused on users' perspective.)

### A Potential solution
Java comes with a Annotation Processor API that allows you to generate new source files at compile time.
Note: 
1. it can only generate new files, cannot modify existing files (*). 
2. This is done by Java compiler, doesn't need any support from editors or IDEs.

## Option 2: `EchoRemote`

If you annotate the original class with `@RayRemote`, the annotation processor will generate a remote version:
```java
class EchoRemote {
    public static RayObject<Integer> echo(int x) { ... }
    public static RayObject<Integer> echo(RayObject<Integer> x) { ... }
}
```
Then you'll use it like this:
```java
RayObject<Integer> res = EchoRemote.echo(1);
System.out.println(res);
```

Here's a prototype of this idea: https://github.com/raulchen/ray_annotation_test
Original file: https://github.com/raulchen/ray_annotation_test/blob/master/annotation_test/src/main/java/test/Adder.java 
Call-site: https://github.com/raulchen/ray_annotation_test/blob/master/annotation_test/src/main/java/Main.java

### Pros:
1. API looks more natural and elegant.
2. Implementation is cleaner, safer and easier to debug.

### Con
1. Some of us don't like the fact that the class name is different.

## Option 3: `x.y.z.remote.Echo`
One way to avoid changing class name would be moving the new class to a different package. Let's say the original package name is `x.y.z`, the new package will be `x.y.z.remote`. However,
1. If you want to use both classes in the same file, you'll need to write full names, because Java doesn't support name alias.
2. Although the names are the same, but the classes are different. It could cause a bug if one wants to compare the classes.

### Option N
We'd like to hear your voice if you have a better solution :) 


(*): Technically, modifying existing code is possible, but it needs to use internal API, requires considerable amount of work, and has some effects (related discussion can be found at https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3852091/is-it-safe-to-use-project-lombok). Comparing the benefits and drawbacks, we don't want to list it as an option here.

issue_title: Test examples applications in continuous integration.
issue_body: Currently, it's possible to change the codebase and break the example applications without triggering any kind of test failure. So we end up testing the example applications by hand. We should do this automatically in Jenkins.

issue_title: jemalloc as Arrow's default allocator
issue_body:
I just wanted to give you a heads-up that we plan to activate `jemalloc` as the default allocator in the Arrow 0.9 release again. This should speed up a lot of Arrow's operations. Previously we had some problems that are now fixed in jemalloc (Arrow will vendor its own namespaced version of jemalloc to ensure that all the fixes are included).

It would be nice if you could try out the latest Arrow HEAD built with `-DARROW_JEMALLOC=ON` and see if this causes any problems for Ray. This would give me some advanced warning if there is still anything problematic. I don't expect anything to actually break but I want to be super-sure ;)

issue_title: Add pool functionality
issue_body:
This utility that `multiprocessing` provides is quite nice:
```
    with closing(multiprocessing.Pool()) as pool:
        X = pool.map(fn, [os.urandom(4) for _ in range(num_trials)])
        episode_rewards = np.hstack(X)
```

We can add something exactly the same into `ray.experimental`.

issue_title: Feature Request: use Twitch stream title in player title bar / save filename
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [x] This is a feature request.
- [x] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Right now, when streaming a Twitch stream via streamlink, the vlc title bar is left with its default value, usually something mysterious like "http://127.0.0.1:46821/".

Twitch's API publishes the current "title" of a stream, which is metadata set by the streamer.  (I see it in the v5 API by querying "streams/?channel=<id>" and looking in response['streams'][0]['channel']['status'].)  This is usually relevant information, and it'd be nice if streamlink furnished it to the user.

I suggest two specific features:

* streamlink should set the title bar of vlc using the "--input-title-format" parameter.  The default value should be something like "{streamer_name} • {channel_status}".  Furthermore, It'd be nice if this were user-adjustable via streamlink's command line with some sort of template.
* streamlink should use the filename specified with -o as a template, parameterized with fields like the name of the streamer, the current title of the channel, and perhaps the current time.  Obviously this should use the same parameterization as the vlc title bar template, if you add that feature too.

issue_title: Framework adaptor only connects first tainted arg to following nodes
issue_body:
In `get_func_cfg_with_tainted_args` the following taints the args of a framework function, and then links the first arg to the following nodes:

https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/a762e00b757b27d3e20f7938f206f987965c5a86/pyt/framework_adaptor.py#L41-L52

For a framework function where multiple args are user-controlled, this could miss issues related to second or subsequent args. For example, in Django, URL path elements may be passed to a View as args.

For example `/xss1/<param>/` could route to:
```
def xss1(request, param):
    return render(request, 'templates/xss.html', {'param': param})
```

The suggested fix is to connect each tainted node to the following node in the for loop:
```
        ...
        func_cfg.nodes.insert(1, tainted_node)
        tainted_node.connect(first_node_after_args)
```

issue_title: Clarification about on_delete in Django 2.0
issue_body:
Will `ParentalKey` require the `on_delete` argument to be set when Django 2.0 support is implemented? Reflecting the requirement for `ForeignKey`?

It might be good to clarify the default in the readme if it is not required and will continue to default to `models.CASCADE`. Also, this might be a factor to consider for Django 2.0 support in general.

**Context**

Django 2.0 will require `on_delete` to be set for ForeignKey.

> A many-to-one relationship. Requires two positional arguments: the class to which the model is related and the on_delete option.
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/models/fields/#foreignkey

Django 1.11 did not require it, and set it to the default `models.CASCADE`
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/fields/#foreignkey


**Related issues**

Django 2.0 support - #85 

Wagtail query about `on_delete` not in docs, but throwing error when not provided to `ParentalKey` - https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/issues/4126

issue_title: Incompatibility with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5
issue_body:
It appears that the library is incompatible with Python versions lower than 3.5, despite claiming to be [fully Python 3 compatible](https://github.com/chainside/btcpy/blob/master/setup.py#L26).

The issue stems from the following idiom mainly:

```python
 m, *pubkeys, n = args
```

Unpacking rules were much stricter prior to Python 3.5 and did not allow this type of argument unpacking where a single argument follows star arguments.

In order for the library to be compatible with Python 3 versions lower than 3.5, it'd have to use the following idiom instead:

```python
 m, n, *pubkeys = args
```

This appears to happen in quite a few places. Python 3.4 compatibility would be nice because that version of Python 3 often comes preinstalled with many systems.

Reference: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0448/

issue_title: Custom scales for sliders
issue_body:
Often when using sliders to manipulate some algorithm parameters it is useful to have logarithmic scale, instead of linear. Now I guess the only way is to manually apply the transformation in the handler, e.g. have the slider from -3 to 3 and in handler write `value = 10**value`. One thing is that it's not really convenient (code defining the real slider range becomes split into two parts in entirely different places), and another is that the slider label of course shows the "raw" value, not the real one used in computations.

I think there is a relatively easy solution - add two function arguments for `FloatSlider` to convert back and forth between the slider raw value and the real one (in my example they would be `lambda x: 10**x` and `lambda x: log10(x)`, and/or maybe a boolean argument `log` for convenience, as probably logarithmic scale is needed way more often than other custom ones.

issue_title: Are floats really a good enough representation for trio times?
issue_body:
Trio's timekeeping is concerned entirely with monotonic durations, not absolute wallclock times, so we don't have to worry about a lot of complexities that wallclocks introduces; in principle we just need a way to represent a simple real number. But a `float` is not a real number. As [PEP 564](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0564/) points out, computer clocks commonly have nanosecond precision these days, but using floats to represent time has less-than-nanosecond precision when dealing with time spans of more than 104 days [or 97 days is what I get for the same calculation... weird. Still, the basic principle is certainly sound]. To handle this, PEP 564 adds new timing functions like `time.monotonic_ns` that use nanosecond integers. These are available starting in 3.7.

Does Trio care? It's not clear. `epoll_wait` only has millisecond precision, and our clock on Windows currently has worse-than-millisecond precision (#33), and `GetQueuedCompletionStatusEx` has ms precision. OTOH, `kevent` takes a `struct timespec` which nominally has nanosecond precision, and it's possible to get higher precision on Linux using a `timerfd`.

We should also consider OS scheduling latency (the gap between when we asked the OS to wake us up, and when it actually wakes us up) and trio scheduler latency (the gap between when the OS wakes us up, and when trio has finished fiddling around in its scheduler and started actually running a task).

For regular processes running on untuned desktop systems with default settings, scheduling latency is often in the millisecond to tens of millisecond range, IIRC. (Maybe I should double-check in case this has changed.) OTOH, I know that if you set up Linux right (e.g. running your process at RT priority, and perhaps using the RT kernel), you can easily get OS scheduling accuracy down into the single-digit microsecond range. This isn't Trio's main use case, but it's a real enough case that maybe we don't want to rule it out at the public API level. OTOH, AFAIK we're still talking about thousands of nanoseconds.

Trio scheduling latency: at least on CPython it's completely impossible to get this down below tens of nanoseconds, because literally every operation in CPython takes that long (e.g. on my laptop creating an empty list is ~20 ns, an empty function call is ~70 ns, accessing an object attribute is ~50 ns, etc.). So getting the latency down to 100 ns would be pretty impressive, and the difference between, say, 1 ns and 2 ns precision in our deadlines is definitely not meaningful. Of course, on PyPy potentially things can go faster...  but for comparison, this is a 2.6 GHz chip, so when the CPU is running at nominal speed a single clock cycle is ~0.38 ns, L1 is a few ns away, etc.

How much accuracy do we lose from using floats? It's hard to know exactly because it depends on the actual value of our clock times – floating point means that larger values get lower precision. Normally we use an epoch that's something approximating boot time, and we can expect all values to be within, say, a year or two of zero. There's no particular guarantee of that, though. If our values are at ~1 year, then a float has a resolution of ~4 ns:

```python
In [55]: t = float(365 * 24 * 60 * 60); (np.nextafter(t, float("inf")) - t) * 1e9
Out[55]: 3.725290298461914
```

OTOH, if we use the standard Unix epoch, then a float currently has a resolution of ~240 ns:

```python
In [57]: t = time.time(); (np.nextafter(t, float("inf")) - t) * 1e9
Out[57]: 238.4185791015625
```

and in 2038 the resolution will drop by a factor of two, becoming ~477 ns. Then in 2016 it'll drop to ~1 µs, which will last until 2242. So even if we were using the Unix epoch for some reason, 64-bit floating point would let us set wakeups with µs precision until 2242, and µs precision is about the most we can currently hope for anyway given OS constraints.

Are there any other reasons we might want high precision? It's nice to be able to give things unique timestamps without collisions... but we can't realistically call `current_time()` twice within, say, 100 ns, and an uptime clock retains that much precision for ~15 years. Plus the use of `MockClock` for testing means systems will probably want to be robust against non-advancing time anyway.

What if there's some unforeseen reason why in the future, we decide we need to increase precision, to nanoseconds or, I dunno, picoseconds? I think it should be pretty easy to increase precision: we would want to change the `Clock` interface to use nanoseconds or whatever, but that's straightforward (and we could shim old `Clock` implementations easily enough: `if not hasattr(clock, "current_time_ns"): clock = OldClockToNewClock(clock)`). And the other three primitive places time is exposed are `cancel_scope.deadline`, `current_time`, and `current_effective_deadline`. In each case we could expose `*_ns` variant, and keep the old one as a shim that converts between the old float seconds representation and the new integer nanoseconds (or whatever) representation.

So I think we don't need to worry about this for now, and this can be closed.

issue_title: Run directories
issue_body:
Workflows in production are often run in the same directory multiple times. Various log files, checkpoint files, submit scripts, config options etc are often produced and ideally stored in a manner that would allow the user to easily identify files associated with a single workflow run. Having a run specific directory would also help #39 and #48. 

I propose that we have run directory that is named runNNN by default and placed in the current directory. Users would have the option to specify a different run directory path, if they so choose.

issue_title: Add mtime Feature for sr_poll
issue_body:
Currently sr_poll just loops through a directory, Compares the results with its cache and does a post on the difference. We have encountered issues where sr_poll is polling a file that hasn't fully been transferred into the directory. The notification is posted and a sr_sarra instance may grab the incomplete file.
​
Metpx sundew receivers have an 'mtime' feature where you can specify how old a file must be (i.e. the time since it last has been written to) before being collected. This feature would be nice to have in sr_poll as well.
​
In addition some files come in and have umasks to restrict permissions before the file has been transferred, however sr_poll will still poll these files and post them. Ignoring those files would be beneficial as well.

was: https://sourceforge.net/p/metpx/feature-requests/53/

issue_title: Run flow tests without root access.
issue_body:
In Dev.rst, the procedure for running the flow_test is described. The flow test downloads data from publically available sources, and then passes them from one component to the next to exercise basic functionality of all of them. Administrative access is used to install the rabbitmq broker, and install the packages (python3-sarracenia, and sarrac).  Requiring Administrative access is a barrier for many developers in managed environments, so limiting the need for it is a worthwhile goal.  Also, there have been situations where we wanted certain users to verify functionality of an instance other than the system installed default one.

See https://github.com/MetPX/sarracenia/blob/master/doc/Dev.rst#testing
for more detail on flow_tests.

location of the scripts:  sarracenia/test
   - flow_setup.sh -> defines exchanges, queues etc... starts the flow rolling.
   - flow_check.sh -> watches the flows until a set number of products have gone through (default: 1000), then stops the shovels at the head of the flows to let them drain, and calculates whether stuff is working.
  - ./flow_cleanup.sh -> reset to an initial state (blows away all working files, configs, queues, etc...)

All of them likely need modification.

The flow_test requires it's own broker.  The test will erase configurations on the broker, so there is no way around using administrative access to install the broker locally, and the dev must have administrative access to the broker (just define the admin user.)   Use of a shared broker by someon running a flow test will end in tears.  So pre-requisite is to install the broker itself, and give the dev
the admin account.

I'm not super familiar with pythonic ways of doing things... there might be a cleaner way to do this involving virtualenv or some such. 

I will describe one approach that makes sense to me (based on the gotchas we hit last month with the HPC mirroring case trying to use non-system sarra):

Currently, the test tests the version that is installed on the system.  It invokes binaries such as *sr* and *sr_shovel* to manipulate configurations. Ideally, one would set a variable only for this case that would be prefixed to invocations of components.  say...  SARRA_LIB
the python code would test for the existence of the SARRA_LIB environment variable.  If present, prepend the invocation of the binary ( subprocess.Popen in sr_instances.py ) with the SARRA_LIB directory.

Additionally the Python distutils (aka setup.py) creates separate entry points that lack the .py suffix.  In normal cases, one invokes  *sr_subscribe*  but in the LIB directory, only sr_subscribe.py exists.  So the invocation will also include appending .py to the end of the component name.
Additionally, on needs to test if the SARRA_LIB directory is in the PYTHONPATH already, and, if not, add it, so that import options from the scripts will be satisfied by the correct files.

So before the change, and example command invocation would be: [ "sr_subscribe", "-no", "1", "start", "myconfig.conf" ]
After the change: [ "/home/peter/src/sarracenia/sarra/sr_subscribe.py", "-no", "1", "start", "myconfig.conf" ] (and PYTHONPATH is set in the environment.)

The C version of some components ( https://github.com/MetPX/Sarrac ) are also part of the flow_test, which is assumed installed in the system.  To avoid that, perhaps a second Environment variable SARRAC_LIB would point to that. or one could just use a convention that the C is found at ${SARRA_LIB}/../sarrac.

Probably a separate environment variable is better.  so for invocations of sr_cpost and sr_cpump, prepend SARRAC_LIB, instead of SARRA_LIB, and don't add the .py suffix.   Also needed for when setting LD_PRELOAD on flow_setup.sh

issue_title: Square Buttons
issue_body: Suggestion: consider including also a quite bigger version of icons, for square-like big buttons, where the icon will be above the text.

issue_title: Wrong parse bug
issue_body:
I found a [bug](http://nodeca.github.com/js-yaml/#yaml=CmJ1Z3M6CiAgeDogMSAjIG9rCiAgeTogMiAjIGJ1Z2d5LCBleHBlY3Qga2V5ICd5JwogIG46IDMgIyBidWdneSwgZXhwZWN0IGtleSAnbicKICB5ZXNWYWx1ZTogeSAjIGJ1Z2d5LCBleHBlY3Qgc3RyaW5nICd5JwogIG5vVmFsdWU6IG4gIyBidWdneSwgZXhwZWN0IHN0cmluZyAnbicKIAogIA==) Keys or values `y` evaluate as true (yes), the same bug with `n`: 

bugs:
  x: 1 # ok
  y: 2 # buggy, expect key 'y'
  n: 3 # buggy, expect key 'n'
  yesValue: y # buggy, expect string 'y'
  noValue: n # buggy, expect string 'n'
-> 
{ bugs: 
   { x: 1,
     true: 2,
     false: 3,
     yesValue: true,
     noValue: false } }

issue_title: Try to access Dexterity in Plone Configuration: Insufficient Privileges
issue_body:
Installed:
Plone 4.1.3 (4112)
Dexterity 1.1 (known good set)
EasyNewsletter 2.6.1

If you activate EasyNewsletter 2.6.1, then Dexterity is not anymore accessible in the Plone Cofiguration. You will get "Insufficient Privileges". After deactivating EasyNewsletter Dexterity is accessible again.
